abstract: Electrochemical ammonia synthesis via the nitrate reduction reaction (NO3RR) has emerged as a compelling alternative to the carbon-intensive Haber-Bosch process. However, while most current research targets the low nitrate concentrations found in wastewater, the high-concentration regimes typical of industrial fertilizer and nuclear waste remain largely unaddressed. Achieving high-rate production in these concentrated electrolytes (≥1 M) is traditionally limited by sluggish hydrogen transfer kinetics. Here, we present a synergistic Ru/Cu₂O cocatalytic system capable of driving NO3RR at a high current of 10.0 A within a 16 cm² flow electrolyzer, achieving 100% Faradaic efficiency toward ammonia in 1 M nitrate. Through deuterium labeling and *operando* Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, we characterize the intermediate species and quantify the accelerated hydrogen transfer rates on the Ru/Cu₂O surface. Furthermore, *ab initio* molecular dynamics (AIMD) simulations reveal that adsorbed hydroxide on the Ru nanoparticles reorganizes the interfacial hydrogen-bonded water network, increasing its density near the Cu₂O surface to facilitate rapid proton relay. This work underscores the critical role of engineering multimetallic interfaces to overcome kinetic bottlenecks in high-rate electrosynthesis.
